abstract: This Anthropic report analyzes a survey of approximately 81,000 Claude.ai users to understand how their experiences and concerns about AI map onto observed patterns of AI usage across occupations. Using Claude-powered classifiers to infer respondent attributes (occupation, career stage, productivity gains, displacement concerns) from open-ended responses, the authors find that workers in occupations with higher observed AI exposure report more job displacement concerns, while high- and low-wage workers report the largest productivity gains—primarily through expanded task scope. The study connects qualitative self-reports to the quantitative usage data tracked by Anthropic's Economic Index.
key_claims: Self-reported concerns about AI-driven job displacement correlate with the level of observed AI exposure in a respondent's occupation.
Early-career workers express significantly higher concern about job displacement than senior workers.
The highest- and lowest-paid occupational groups report the largest productivity gains from AI use.
Productivity gains accrue most often to users themselves rather than to employers, clients, or AI companies—though this pattern is weaker for early-career workers.
The relationship between reported speedup from AI and perceived job threat is U-shaped: both those slowed down by AI and those most sped up by it report higher displacement concerns.
Expanded scope (doing new tasks) is the most commonly cited form of productivity gain, slightly more common than speed.
methods: Open-ended online survey of 80,508 Claude.ai personal-account users (referred to as 81,000).

Use of Claude-powered LLM classifiers to infer respondent attributes (occupation, career stage) and code sentiments (job threat, productivity gain magnitude, type of gain, beneficiary of gains) from free-text transcripts.
A 1–7 productivity rating scale ranging from 'less productive' to 'transformatively more productive.'
Linking inferred occupations to the 'Observed Exposure' measure from Massenkoff and McCrory (2026), reflecting the share of an occupation's tasks for which Claude is used.
Linear fits and quartile comparisons to relate exposure, speedup, wage, and career stage to displacement concern and productivity outcomes.
Robustness check restricting analysis to the 11% of respondents who explicitly stated their occupation.
findings: About one-fifth of respondents voiced concern about economic displacement from AI.

A 10-percentage-point increase in observed exposure is associated with a 1.3-percentage-point increase in perceived job threat; the top exposure quartile reported displacement concerns three times as often as the bottom quartile.
Mean productivity rating was 5.1 ('substantially more productive'); only 3% reported negative or neutral impacts and 42% gave no clear productivity indication.
Management occupations (largely entrepreneurs/solopreneurs) and computer/math occupations reported the highest productivity gains; legal and scientific professions reported the mildest gains.
48% of respondents mentioning productivity cited scope expansion; 40% cited speed; smaller shares cited quality or cost.
Among respondents identifying a beneficiary of AI gains, most named themselves; ~10% named employers/clients; smaller shares named AI companies or described net negatives.
80% of senior professionals reported personal benefits from AI, versus 60% of early-career workers.
Occupation could be inferred for 39% of respondents (11% explicitly stated, 28% inferred from context).
contributions: Provides one of the first large-scale linkages between qualitative user-reported experiences of AI and quantitative occupational exposure measures.
Offers empirical evidence that worker perceptions of AI displacement risk track measured AI diffusion patterns.
Demonstrates a methodology for using LLM classifiers to extract structured economic variables (occupation, career stage, productivity type) from open-ended interview data at scale.
Extends Anthropic's Economic Index by adding a worker-sentiment dimension to its task-based usage analysis.
Surfaces hypotheses—such as the U-shaped relationship between speedup and displacement concern—that could be tested in future structured surveys.
framing: The paper situates itself within Anthropic's ongoing Economic Index research program, which measures AI's economic footprint via observed Claude usage. It positions qualitative survey data as a complement to task-based exposure measures in the broader economics-of-AI and labor-displacement literature, building on prior findings about slowdowns in early-career hiring and the distributional impacts of AI productivity gains.
